r3_text: Internal hemorrhoids are painful.
r3_info: Above pectinate line; insensitive to pain.
r4_text: The external anal sphincter is supplied by the inferior rectal nerve.
r5_text: Lymph from below the pectinate line drains to the superficial inguinal nodes.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: The tunica vaginalis is a remnant of the processus vaginalis.
r2_text: The pampiniform plexus helps in thermoregulation.
r3_text: The lymphatic drainage of the testis is to the inguinal nodes.
r4_text: The cremasteric reflex is mediated by the genitofemoral nerve.
r5_text: The left testicular vein drains into the IVC.
r5_info: Drains into the left renal vein.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: Osteoblasts secrete the organic matrix (osteoid).
r2_text: Osteocytes reside in small spaces called lacunae.
r3_text: Canaliculi allow communication between osteocytes.
r4_text: The periosteum covers the articular surfaces of bones.
r4_info: Articular surfaces are covered by hyaline cartilage.
r5_text: Woven bone is more mechanically sound than lamellar bone.
r5_info: Lamellar bone is mature and stronger.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: Exocrine glands secrete products into ducts.
r2_text: Holocrine secretion involves the death of the secretory cell.
r3_text: Merocrine glands lose a portion of their cytoplasm during secretion.
r3_info: Merocrine uses exocytosis; Apocrine loses cytoplasm.
r4_text: The thyroid is an example of a follicular endocrine gland.
r5_text: Goblet cells are characterized by an abundance of RER and Golgi.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: The ductus venosus shunts blood from the umbilical vein to the IVC.
r2_text: The foramen ovale connects the two ventricles.
r2_info: Connects the right atrium to the left atrium.
r3_text: High oxygen levels cause the ductus arteriosus to close after birth.
r4_text: The umbilical arteries carry oxygenated blood to the fetus.
r4_info: They carry deoxygenated blood to the placenta.
r5_text: The ligamentum teres is the remnant of the umbilical vein.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: The first arch is the mandibular arch.
r2_text: The facial nerve is the nerve of the second arch.
r3_text: The third arch forms the stylopharyngeus muscle.
r4_text: The fourth and sixth arches are innervated by the vagus nerve.
r5_text: The second arch gives rise to the greater horn of the hyoid.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: GLUT1 is the primary transporter in the brain and RBCs.
r2_text: GLUT2 has a high Km for glucose.
r2_info: Found in liver and pancreas; acts as glucose sensor.
r3_text: GLUT4 is insulin-dependent.
r3_info: Found in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue.
r4_text: SGLT1 is a passive transporter.
r4_info: It is a secondary active symporter with sodium.
r5_text: GLUT5 is specific for fructose transport.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: GAGs are unbranched polysaccharide chains.
r2_text: Heparin acts as a natural anticoagulant.
r3_text: Chondroitin sulfate is the most abundant GAG in the body.
r4_text: Keratan sulfate contains uronic acid.
r4_info: It is the only GAG that lacks uronic acid.
r5_text: They are highly negatively charged.
r5_info: Due to sulfate and carboxyl groups.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: The ninhydrin reaction is used to detect amino acids.
r2_text: Oxidative deamination of glutamate produces alpha-ketoglutarate.
r3_text: Decarboxylation of histidine produces histamine.
r4_text: Glutathione is a tripeptide containing glycine glutamate and cysteine.
r5_text: The Sanger's reagent identifies the C-terminal amino acid.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Regarding specialized products of amino acids:
r1_text: Tyrosine is the precursor for melanin.
r2_text: Creatine is synthesized from arginine and glycine.
r3_text: Nitric oxide is derived from lysine.
r4_text: GABA is a derivative of glutamate.
r5_text: Thyroid hormones (T3 T4) are derived from tyrosine.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: mRNA carries the genetic code from DNA to ribosomes.
r2_text: tRNA has a cloverleaf secondary structure.
r3_text: rRNA is the most abundant type of RNA in the cell.
r4_text: Small nuclear RNA (snRNA) is involved in splicing.
r5_text: miRNA stimulates the translation of specific mRNAs.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: A silent mutation changes the amino acid sequence.
r1_info: Changes the codon but not the amino acid.
r2_text: A nonsense mutation creates a premature stop codon.
r3_text: UV light causes thymine dimers.
r4_text: Xeroderma pigmentosum is a defect in nucleotide excision repair.
r5_text: Mismatch repair corrects errors made during DNA replication.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Acetoacetate is a ketone body.
r2_text: Ketogenesis occurs when Acetyl-CoA levels are high.
r3_text: The brain can use ketone bodies during prolonged starvation.
r4_text: Acetone is used as an energy source by the heart.
r4_info: Acetone is a waste product excreted via breath.
r5_text: Ketolysis occurs in the liver.
r5_info: The liver lacks the enzyme thiophorase.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Prostaglandins are synthesized from arachidonic acid.
r2_text: Leukotrienes are involved in asthma and inflammation.
r3_text: Thromboxane A2 promotes platelet aggregation.
r4_text: Prostacyclin (PGI2) inhibits platelet aggregation.
r5_text: NSAIDs stimulate prostaglandin synthesis.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: It produces NADH for the electron transport chain.
r1_info: It produces NADPH for reductive biosynthesis.
r2_text: The oxidative phase is reversible.
r3_text: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) is the rate-limiting enzyme.
r4_text: It provides ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis.
r5_text: G6PD deficiency leads to hemolytic anemia.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Glycogen synthase is the rate-limiting enzyme of glycogenesis.
r2_text: Glycogen phosphorylase requires pyridoxal phosphate (PLP).
r3_text: Von Gierke disease is a deficiency of alpha-glucosidase.
r3_info: It is a deficiency of Glucose-6-Phosphatase.
r4_text: Epinephrine stimulates glycogenolysis in both liver and muscle.
r5_text: Muscle glycogen contributes to blood glucose levels.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Regarding the metabolism of individual amino acids:
r1_text: Maple Syrup Urine Disease is a defect in branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase.
r2_text: Homocystinuria is often caused by a deficiency of cystathionine beta-synthase.
r3_text: Albinism is due to a lack of phenylalanine hydroxylase.
r4_text: Tryptophan is the precursor for Vitamin B3 (Niacin).
r5_text: Arginine is the precursor for the gasotransmitter Nitric Oxide.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Glutamine is the major non-toxic carrier of ammonia in the blood.
r2_text: The glucose-alanine cycle transports ammonia from muscle to liver.
r2_info: Alanine is converted back to pyruvate in the liver.
r3_text: Ammonia is highly toxic to the central nervous system.
r4_text: Glutaminase in the kidney releases ammonia for pH buffering.
r5_text: Hyperammonemia can be caused by liver cirrhosis.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Vitamin B12 absorption requires intrinsic factor from parietal cells.
r2_text: Fat-soluble vitamins (A D E K) require micelles for absorption.
r3_text: Vitamin C is absorbed via sodium-dependent transporters (SVCT).
r4_text: Folic acid is absorbed in the duodenum and jejunum.
r5_text: Most water-soluble vitamins are absorbed in the stomach.
r5_info: Most are absorbed in the small intestine.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Cellulose is a soluble fiber.
r2_text: Lignin is a carbohydrate.
r3_text: Fiber decreases the transit time of food in the gut.
r4_text: Soluble fibers can lower blood cholesterol levels.
r5_text: Pectin is commonly found in fruits.
r5_info: A soluble fiber used in jellies.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Adding isotonic saline to the ECF increases ECF volume but does not change osmolarity.
r1_info: No shift between ECF and ICF.
r2_text: Adding pure water to the ECF increases both ECF and ICF volumes.
r3_text: Excessive sweating leads to ICF dehydration.
r3_info: ECF becomes hypertonic drawing water from ICF.
r4_text: Infusion of hypertonic saline causes the ICF volume to increase.
r5_text: The Gibbs-Donnan effect influences the distribution of ions across the capillary wall.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Metabolic acidosis is characterized by a low blood pH and low bicarbonate.
r2_text: In respiratory alkalosis the primary change is a decrease in PCO2.
r3_text: The anion gap is increased in lactic acidosis.
r4_text: Renal compensation for respiratory acidosis involves increasing H+ secretion.
r5_text: Vomiting typically causes metabolic acidosis.

subject: Physiology
r1_text: The cell membrane is a fluid mosaic of lipids and proteins.
r2_text: Cholesterol increases the fluidity of the membrane at high temperatures.
r3_text: Integral proteins span the entire lipid bilayer.
r4_text: The glycocalyx is found on the inner surface of the membrane.
r5_text: Flip-flop movement of phospholipids is a frequent occurrence.
r5_info: It is rare compared to lateral diffusion.

subject: Physiology
r1_text: Mitochondria are the primary site of ATP production.
r2_text: Rough endoplasmic reticulum is involved in lipid synthesis.
r3_text: Lysosomes contain hydrolases that work at acidic pH.
r4_text: The Golgi apparatus modifies and packages proteins for secretion.
r4_info: The shipping center of the cell.
r5_text: Peroxisomes are involved in the breakdown of very long-chain fatty acids.

subject: Physiology
stem: Regarding the nucleus and cell cycle:
r1_text: The nucleolus is the site of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) synthesis.
r2_text: DNA replication occurs during the G1 phase of the cell cycle.
r3_text: Mitosis results in two genetically identical daughter cells.
r4_text: In prophase the nuclear envelope breaks down.
r5_text: Apoptosis is an inflammatory process of cell death.

subject: Physiology
stem: Regarding transport across the cell membrane:
r1_text: Simple diffusion requires carrier proteins.
r2_text: Facilitated diffusion is a saturable process.
r2_info: Limited by the number of carriers.
r3_text: Primary active transport uses energy directly from ATP hydrolysis.
r4_text: Osmosis is the movement of solute from low to high concentration.
r5_text: Exocytosis is the process of secreting substances out of the cell.
r5_info: Vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: The primary structure is determined by peptide bonds.
r2_text: Alpha-helices are stabilized by hydrogen bonds between R-groups.
r2_info: H-bonds are between backbone carbonyl and amide.
r3_text: Tertiary structure describes the overall 3D folding of a single polypeptide.
r4_text: Denaturation involves the breaking of peptide bonds.
r5_text: Quaternary structure is present in all proteins.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Myoglobin has a higher affinity for oxygen than hemoglobin.
r1_info: Acts as an oxygen store in muscle.
r2_text: Hemoglobin shows sigmoidal oxygen dissociation curve.
r3_text: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) has a lower affinity for 2-3-BPG.
r3_info: Allows it to bind O2 more strongly.
r4_text: The Bohr effect shifts the Hb curve to the left.
r5_text: Carbon monoxide binds to hemoglobin with much higher affinity than oxygen.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Enzymes increase the rate of reaction by lowering activation energy.
r2_text: The Michaelis constant (Km) is the substrate concentration at Vmax.
r2_info: It is the concentration at half Vmax.
r3_text: A competitive inhibitor increases the Km but doesn't change Vmax.
r4_text: Allosteric enzymes follow Michaelis-Menten kinetics.
r5_text: Vmax is directly proportional to enzyme concentration.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Collagen is a triple helical fibrous protein.
r2_text: Albumin is the major contributor to plasma oncotic pressure.
r3_text: Immunoglobulins are composed of two heavy and two light chains.
r4_text: Casein is the primary protein found in wheat.
r4_info: Gluten is in wheat; Casein is in milk.
r5_text: Ferritin is the primary storage form of iron.

subject: Physiology
r1_text: The P wave represents atrial depolarization.
r2_text: The QRS complex represents ventricular repolarization.
r3_text: The PR interval includes the AV node delay.
r4_text: The ST segment is normally elevated in a healthy individual.
r5_text: A prolonged QT interval can predispose to arrhythmias.

subject: Physiology
r1_text: Renin is secreted by the macula densa cells.
r2_text: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) is primarily found in the lungs.
r3_text: Angiotensin II causes systemic vasodilation.
r4_text: Aldosterone promotes potassium secretion in the collecting ducts.
r5_text: Renin release is stimulated by decreased renal perfusion pressure.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile.
r2_text: The common bile duct is formed by the union of the right and left hepatic ducts.
r2_info: Formed by common hepatic and cystic ducts.
r3_text: The sphincter of Oddi controls the flow of bile into the duodenum.
r3_info: Located at the major duodenal papilla.
r4_text: The fundus of the gallbladder typically projects below the inferior border of the liver.
r4_info: Usually at the tip of the 9th costal cartilage.
r5_text: Cholecystokinin (CCK) causes relaxation of the gallbladder wall.

subject: Anatomy
r1_text: The neurovascular bundle runs between the internal and innermost intercostal muscles.
r2_text: The intercostal vein is the most inferior structure in the costal groove.
r2_info: Order is Vein Artery Nerve from superior to inferior.
r3_text: The anterior intercostal arteries of the upper spaces arise from the internal thoracic artery.
r3_info: A branch of the subclavian artery.
r4_text: The external intercostal muscle membrane replaces the muscle layer anteriorly.
r5_text: The typical intercostal nerve supplies both motor and sensory fibers.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: HDL is responsible for reverse cholesterol transport.
r1_info: Returns cholesterol from tissues to liver.
r2_text: LDL has the highest protein content among lipoproteins.
r2_info: HDL has the highest protein content.
r3_text: VLDL is synthesized primarily in the intestinal mucosa.
r4_text: Apolipoprotein B-100 is the major structural protein of LDL.
r5_text: Chylomicrons transport dietary triglycerides.
r5_info: Cleared by lipoprotein lipase in capillaries.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Vitamin A is a steroid derivative of cholesterol.
r2_text: Vitamin D undergoes its first hydroxylation in the kidney.
r2_info: First hydroxylation is in the liver to 25-OH D3.
r3_text: Vitamin E acts as a biological antioxidant.
r3_info: Protects cell membranes from lipid peroxidation.
r4_text: Vitamin K is required for the gamma-carboxylation of glutamate residues.
r4_info: Essential for clotting factors II VII IX X.
r5_text: Toxicity is less common in fat-soluble vitamins than water-soluble vitamins.
r5_info: They accumulate in body fat leading to higher toxicity risk.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: Thiamine (B1) is a coenzyme for pyruvate dehydrogenase.
r2_text: Niacin (B3) deficiency classically causes scurvy.
r3_text: Riboflavin (B2) is a structural component of FAD.
r4_text: Pyridoxine (B6) is required for transamination reactions.
r4_info: Forms the active coenzyme pyridoxal phosphate.
r5_text: Biotin (B7) is a carrier of one-carbon units in methylation reactions.
r5_info: It is a carrier of CO2 in carboxylation reactions.

subject: Biochemistry
r1_text: It is a coenzyme in the hydroxylation of proline and lysine.
r1_info: Essential for proper collagen triple helix synthesis.
r2_text: Humans can synthesize Vitamin C from glucose.
r2_info: Humans lack the enzyme gulonolactone oxidase.
r3_text: It facilitates the intestinal absorption of dietary non-heme iron.
r3_info: Keeps iron in the more easily absorbed ferrous state.
r4_text: Severe deficiency leads directly to megaloblastic anemia.
r5_text: It functions as a potent water-soluble antioxidant.
r5_info: Scavenges free radicals in aqueous body compartments.
